agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand signals. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving most critical business parameters undefined.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing / Rate Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time demand-based delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "Undefined metric representing volume of delivery requests relative to capacity — exact measurement unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds unspecified" },
      { "term": "Real-time", "definition": "Pricing adjustments occur dynamically as demand changes, not on a batch/scheduled basis — latency tolerance unspecified" },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "No manual human approval required for individual price adjustments once the system is live" },
      { "term": "Competitive", "definition": "Prices should not significantly exceed market rates such that customers defect to competitors — no benchmark defined" },
      { "term": "Profitable", "definition": "Prices must remain above a cost floor that ensures the company does not lose money on deliveries" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "When demand increases, delivery prices must increase",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy, not a data integrity constraint. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during demand spikes for strategic reasons (e.g., customer retention campaign). The direction of price-demand correlation is a business decision.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand metric rises above the previous observation, the computed price must be >= the previous price (assuming no other inputs changed)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When demand decreases, delivery prices must decrease",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same as BR-1 — this is a pricing policy direction. The business could choose to hold prices sticky-downward to protect margins.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand metric falls below the previous observation, the computed price must be <= the previous price (assuming no other inputs changed)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real-time (not batch/scheduled)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The latency requirement is a business policy choice. The company could decide daily recalculation is sufficient. 'Real-time' is an SLA, not a state integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a change in demand, the system must compute and publish a new price within [UNDEFINED] seconds/minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual approval step",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation level is a business policy. The company could revert to requiring human approval for adjustments above a threshold. A compliance officer could mandate manual review.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a demand change triggers a price recalculation, the new price is applied without any human-in-the-loop approval gate"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A price ceiling guardrail must exist to prevent prices from exceeding a maximum bound",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without an upper bound, a bug or extreme demand spike could produce prices of $10,000 for a local delivery — this is a system integrity constraint. The EXISTENCE of a ceiling is invariant; the specific VALUE is policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed price may ever exceed the configured maximum price ceiling, regardless of demand level"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A price floor guardrail must exist to prevent prices from dropping below the cost of fulfillment",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Pricing below cost means the company loses money on every delivery — this is a financial integrity constraint. Operating at a guaranteed loss per transaction corrupts the business model. The EXISTENCE of a floor is invariant; the specific VALUE is policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed price may ever be less than the configured minimum price floor, regardless of demand level"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically nonsensical — you cannot pay a customer to deliver their package under any normal business model. This is a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "The computed price must always be > 0 in the system's base currency"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The demand signal used for pricing must be based on current observable data, not stale or missing data",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Pricing on stale or null demand data means the system is making decisions on corrupted state. If demand data is unavailable, the system must not silently use a default — this is a data integrity concern.",
      "testable_assertion": "The system must not compute a dynamic price when the demand signal is older than [UNDEFINED freshness threshold] or is missing entirely; it must fall back to a defined safe behavior"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand signal is unavailable or data feed is down", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — CEO did not address failover. System must have a fallback (e.g., last known price, base price, disable dynamic pricing). This is a critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly (e.g., every few seconds)", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — no dampening/smoothing policy specified. Without it, customers may see price flicker, destroying trust." },
    { "scenario": "Demand is exactly at the boundary between 'high' and 'low'", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — no demand thresholds or tiers defined. What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' is completely unspecified." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple regions have different demand levels simultaneously", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — no geographic granularity specified. Is pricing per-city, per-zone, per-route, or global?" },
    { "scenario": "A customer sees one price, but by the time they confirm, demand has changed", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — no price-lock/quote-validity window specified. This is a major UX and trust concern." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail ceiling is hit — demand is extreme and price is capped", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — does the system continue accepting orders at the capped price (potentially at a loss of opportunity), or does it throttle/queue orders?" },
    { "scenario": "Competitor drops prices significantly during a high-demand period", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — 'competitive' was mentioned but no competitor price input is specified. Is this a factor in the algorithm or aspirational?" },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price-gouging laws triggered during emergencies (e.g., natural disaster)", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED — no compliance or regulatory override mechanism mentioned. Many jurisdictions have anti-price-gouging statutes that cap allowable price increases during declared emergencies." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Dynamic pricing can significantly increase revenue during peak demand and improve utilization during off-peak. However, without well-defined guardrails, it can also cause margin erosion (floor too low) or customer churn (ceiling too high). The lack of defined parameters makes financial modeling impossible.",
    "operational": "Fully automated pricing removes manual rate-setting overhead but introduces operational complexity: monitoring, alerting, demand signal reliability, and fallback procedures all become critical operational concerns that are not addressed.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price volatility, lack of quote guarantees, and perception of unfairness (surge pricing backlash, as seen with ride-sharing) are significant UX risks.",
    "risk": "HIGH — Regulatory risk (price-gouging laws), reputational risk (customer backlash to surge pricing), financial risk (algorithm errors causing losses), and competitive risk (pricing customers out of the market). None of these are addressed in the spec.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Will pay variable prices — need transparency, fairness, and price-lock guarantees to maintain trust" },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Need defined margin floors, revenue targets, and reporting on dynamic pricing performance" },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Pricing affects order volume, which affects capacity planning and courier allocation" },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "If pay is tied to delivery price, their compensation becomes variable — labor relations concern" },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review for anti-price-gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and contractual obligations" },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability — need clear policies for disputes and refunds" },
      { "group": "Product/Marketing", "impact": "Must communicate pricing model to customers without triggering backlash — positioning is critical" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 25,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is an informal, high-level vision statement with almost no concrete parameters defined. The core DIRECTION is clear (demand-based dynamic pricing with guardrails), but every critical parameter — demand measurement, price calculation algorithm, guardrail thresholds, geographic scope, temporal granularity, failover behavior, regulatory compliance, quote validity, and competitive input — is undefined. This spec is not implementable without at least 8-10 follow-up conversations. Confidence reflects spec quality, not analysis quality.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices should correlate positively with demand (BR-1, BR-2)",
      "A guardrail mechanism must exist to bound prices (BR-5, BR-6)",
      "The system should be automated, not manual (BR-4)",
      "Prices must be positive values (BR-7)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What 'demand' means — how is it measured, at what granularity?",
      "What 'real-time' means — sub-second? minutes? hourly?",
      "What the guardrail thresholds are — price ceiling and floor values",
      "Geographic scope — global, regional, per-route?",
      "Whether competitor pricing is an input or just aspirational language",
      "Quote validity / price-lock behavior for in-progress orders",
      "Regulatory compliance requirements by jurisdiction",
      "Failover behavior when demand data is unavailable",
      "Whether courier compensation is affected by dynamic pricing"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The system prices delivery fees, not the goods being delivered",
      "There exists a measurable demand signal the system can consume",
      "'Guardrails' implies both upper and lower bounds, though only upper was implied by 'don't go crazy'",
      "The price floor should at minimum cover fulfillment cost to ensure profitability",
      "'Competitive' is an aspirational goal, not a concrete algorithm input tied to competitor data feeds"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The definition of 'demand' is completely unspecified. Is it order volume per hour? Orders-to-couriers ratio? Regional capacity utilization? The entire pricing algorithm depends on this definition, and different definitions produce radically different pricing behaviors.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Finance", "Operations"],
      "options": ["Order volume per time window", "Orders-to-available-couriers ratio", "Capacity utilization percentage", "Composite index of multiple signals"],
      "recommendation": "Demand should be defined as orders-to-available-capacity ratio per pricing zone, as this captures both sides of the supply-demand equation. Requires input from Operations."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrail thresholds are undefined. 'Prices don't go crazy' is not a specification. The price ceiling and floor values, and whether they are absolute amounts or percentage-based multipliers, must be defined.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Legal", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Absolute dollar caps (e.g., $5-$50)", "Multiplier caps (e.g., 0.5x-3x base price)", "Margin-based (e.g., never below 15% margin, never above 200% markup)"],
      "recommendation": "Use multiplier-based guardrails relative to a base price (e.g., 0.7x to 2.5x), as this scales across different delivery types and distances. Finance must define the specific multipliers."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No regulatory or legal review has been mentioned. Dynamic/surge pricing is regulated in many jurisdictions, particularly during emergencies. Anti-price-gouging laws in multiple US states and EU consumer protection directives may constrain the system.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal", "Compliance", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["Proceed without legal review", "Block implementation until legal review is complete", "Implement with conservative guardrails while legal review is in parallel"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review must happen before launch. Implement with conservative guardrails in the interim, including an emergency kill-switch that reverts to base pricing."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No price-lock or quote validity window is defined. If a customer is quoted $12 and the price changes to $18 before they confirm, which price do they pay? This is a critical trust and fairness question.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Product"],
      "options": ["No price lock — price at time of confirmation", "Time-limited quote (e.g., price held for 5 minutes)", "Price locked at time of display until session expires"],
      "recommendation": "Implement a time-limited price lock (e.g., 3-5 minutes) to balance customer trust with pricing accuracy. This is standard practice in airline and ride-share industries."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Geographic granularity of pricing is unspecified. Dynamic pricing in logistics varies dramatically depending on whether it is applied globally, per-city, per-zone, or per-route. This fundamentally shapes the system architecture and the demand signal design.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Operations", "Product"],
      "options": ["Global single price", "Per-city pricing", "Per-zone within city", "Per-origin-destination pair"],
      "recommendation": "Per-zone pricing is the industry standard for last-mile delivery. Requires zone definition from Operations."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
